Bean: Green beans are low-calorie, high in fiber and vitamin K. Best stored in a perforated bag in the fridge crisper, used within 5-7 days. Common prep: steaming, stir-frying, sauteing with garlic.
Bitter_Gourd: Also called bitter melon/karela. Rich in vitamin C and known for compounds that may help regulate blood sugar. Salting and rinsing before cooking reduces bitterness. Store unwashed in the fridge for up to a week.
Bottle_Gourd: Also called lauki/calabash. Very high water content, low calorie, mild flavor. Commonly used in curries, soups, and juices. Store in a cool, dry place or refrigerate; use within a few days once cut.
Brinjal: Also called eggplant/aubergine. Good source of fiber and antioxidants (especially in the skin). Salting slices before cooking reduces bitterness and oil absorption. Store in the fridge crisper, use within 5-7 days.
Broccoli: High in vitamin C, vitamin K, and fiber. Best steamed lightly to preserve nutrients; overcooking causes nutrient loss and a sulfurous smell. Store unwashed in a loose bag in the fridge, use within a week.
Cabbage: High in vitamin C and K, low calorie. Keeps well for weeks in the fridge if kept whole and unwashed. Common prep: stir-fry, slaw, soups, fermenting (sauerkraut/kimchi).
Capsicum: Also called bell pepper. Excellent source of vitamin C, especially red/yellow varieties which are riper and sweeter than green. Store in the fridge crisper for 1-2 weeks.
Carrot: Rich in beta-carotene (vitamin A precursor), which is better absorbed when carrots are cooked with a little fat. Store in the fridge, ideally with tops removed, for 2-4 weeks.
Cauliflower: Good source of vitamin C and fiber, popular as a low-carb rice/potato substitute. Store head-down in a bag in the fridge crisper, use within a week.
Cucumber: About 95% water, mild source of vitamin K. Best stored in the fridge (not near bananas/apples, which speed ripening-related spoilage via ethylene). Use within a week.
Papaya: Technically a fruit, rich in vitamin C and papain, an enzyme that aids digestion. Ripens at room temperature; refrigerate once ripe to slow further softening.
Potato: High in potassium and vitamin C, mostly starch/carbohydrate. Store in a cool, dark, well-ventilated place (not the fridge, which converts starch to sugar and affects taste/texture). Green spots indicate solanine buildup and should be cut away.
Pumpkin: Rich in beta-carotene and fiber. Whole pumpkins keep for months in a cool, dry place; cut pumpkin should be refrigerated and used within a few days.
Radish: Low calorie, decent source of vitamin C, with a peppery bite that mellows when cooked. Store in the fridge with leafy tops removed (tops draw moisture from the root), use within 1-2 weeks.
Tomato: Rich in vitamin C and lycopene (an antioxidant more bioavailable after cooking). Best stored at room temperature out of direct sun for flavor; refrigeration dulls flavor and texture once ripe.
